solution: Locating \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \) on the unit circle and reading its \( y \)-coordinate gives \( \sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the \( x \)-coordinate (cosine) instead of the \( y \)-coordinate (sine).
This has the wrong sign for the quadrant containing this angle.
This drops the numerator \( k\), using the base angle \( \pi/n\) instead of the full angle.
This reports the tangent instead of the sine.
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct value.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct value.

solution: Locating \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \) on the unit circle and reading its \( x \)-coordinate gives \( \cos\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the \( y \)-coordinate (sine) instead of the \( x \)-coordinate (cosine).
This has the wrong sign for the quadrant containing this angle.
This drops the numerator \( k\), using the base angle \( \pi/n\) instead of the full angle.
This reports the secant instead of the cosine.
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct value.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct value.
This reports the cotangent (reciprocal) instead of the tangent.
This has the wrong sign for the quadrant containing this angle.
This reports the sine instead of the tangent.
This reports the cosine instead of the tangent.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value.

stem: Find \( \sec\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \), the reciprocal of cosine, using the unit circle.
solution: \( \sec\theta=\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta} \); at \( \theta=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \), \( \cos\theta \) is read from the unit circle and inverted to give \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the cosine itself instead of its reciprocal.
This has the wrong sign for the quadrant containing this angle.
This reports the cosecant (reciprocal of sine) instead of the secant.
This reports the sine instead of the secant.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value.

stem: Find the reference angle of \( \theta=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \), the positive acute angle between the terminal side of \( \theta \) and the \( x \)-axis.
solution: The reference angle is always the acute angle in \( \left[0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right] \) with the same absolute sine value as \( \theta \), namely \( \arcsin\left|\sin\theta\right|=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the original angle itself without reducing it to an acute reference angle.
This uses the supplementary angle instead of the true reference angle.
This gives a negative angle rather than a positive acute one.
This uses the complementary angle instead of the true reference angle.
This adds a stray multiple of the base angle to the correct reference angle.
This subtracts a stray multiple of the base angle from the correct reference angle.
